summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a two-way table?

A two-way table organizes categorical data into rows and columns, where each cell shows the count for a specific combination of two categorical variables.

What is a 'joint frequency'?

A joint frequency is the count in an inner cell where a specific row category and column category intersect, showing how many subjects belong to both categories.

What is a 'marginal frequency'?

Marginal frequencies are the row totals and column totals that appear along the margins (edges) of the two-way table.

What is a 'conditional frequency'?

A conditional frequency gives the proportion within a specific group (row or column). It answers questions like 'Of the males, what fraction prefer action movies?'

What is a 'contingency table'?

A contingency table is another name for a two-way table, used to display the frequency distribution of two categorical variables simultaneously.

What is an 'association' between two categorical variables?

An association exists when the conditional distributions of one variable change depending on the category of the other variable, suggesting a relationship between them.

A school surveys 200 students about their preferred lunch option (Pizza or Salad) and their grade (Freshman or Senior). The results show 80 Freshmen chose Pizza, 20 Freshmen chose Salad, 50 Seniors chose Pizza, and 50 Seniors chose Salad. What is the joint frequency of Seniors who chose Salad?

The joint frequency for Seniors choosing Salad is 50, as it is the count in the cell where the Senior row and Salad column intersect.

A hospital tracks patient satisfaction (Satisfied or Unsatisfied) by ward (A or B). Ward A: 70 Satisfied, 30 Unsatisfied. Ward B: 45 Satisfied, 55 Unsatisfied. What is the marginal frequency of satisfied patients?

The marginal frequency of satisfied patients is the total of the Satisfied column: 70 + 45 = 115.

A school counselor surveys 180 students about study habits (Study Alone or Study Group) and GPA range (Above 3.0 or Below 3.0). Above 3.0: 50 Alone, 40 Group. Below 3.0: 30 Alone, 60 Group. What is the grand total, and what fraction of all students study alone?

Grand total = 50 + 40 + 30 + 60 = 180. Study Alone total = 50 + 30 = 80. So 80/180 (about 44.4%) of all students study alone.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A university surveys 300 students about housing preference (On-campus or Off-campus) and year (Freshman or Upperclassman). Freshman: 100 On-campus, 50 Off-campus. Upperclassman: 40 On-campus, 110 Off-campus. What percentage of all students prefer on-campus housing?

Total on-campus = 100 + 40 = 140. The relative frequency is 140/300 = 46.7% of all students prefer on-campus housing.

Example: A marketing firm surveys 500 people about brand preference (Brand X or Brand Y) and age group (Under 30 or 30+). Under 30: 150 Brand X, 100 Brand Y. 30+: 80 Brand X, 170 Brand Y. What conditional frequency of Brand X preference exists for the Under 30 group?

The conditional frequency of Brand X given Under 30 is 150 divided by the Under 30 total (150 + 100 = 250), which is 150/250 = 60%.

Example: A gym tracks class attendance by class type (Yoga or Spin) and time (Morning or Evening). Morning: 45 Yoga, 30 Spin. Evening: 20 Yoga, 55 Spin. The manager wants to know if class preference is associated with time of day. What should they compare?

To determine association, compare conditional distributions: Morning is 60% Yoga/40% Spin, while Evening is 27% Yoga/73% Spin. These differ, suggesting an association between class preference and time of day.

Example: A restaurant surveys 250 diners about meal preference (Steak or Fish) and dining time (Lunch or Dinner). Lunch: 40 Steak, 60 Fish. Dinner: 90 Steak, 60 Fish. A manager claims dinner patrons and lunch patrons have identical preferences. Is this correct?

Although both groups have 60 Fish orders, the proportions differ: Lunch is 40/100 = 40% Steak, while Dinner is 90/150 = 60% Steak. The conditional distributions are different.

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

In a two-way table with 60 males and 40 females, the marginal frequency for males is:

The marginal frequency for males is the total count for that row or column category, which is 60.

A conditional frequency is found by dividing a joint frequency by:

A conditional frequency is calculated by dividing a joint frequency by its row or column total, giving a proportion within that specific group.

If 40 out of 60 males prefer action movies, the conditional frequency of action movie preference given male is:

The conditional frequency is the joint frequency (40) divided by the marginal frequency for males (60), giving 40/60 or about 66.7%.

To convert a joint frequency to a relative frequency, divide by:

A relative frequency is found by dividing a joint frequency by the grand total, expressing the count as a proportion or percentage of the entire data set.

What is a 'joint frequency'

A joint frequency is the count in an inner cell where a specific row category and column category intersect, showing how many subjects belong to both categories.

What is a 'marginal frequency'

Marginal frequencies are the row totals and column totals that appear along the margins (edges) of the two-way table.

What is a 'conditional frequency'

A conditional frequency gives the proportion within a specific group (row or column). It answers questions like 'Of the males, what fraction prefer action movies?'

What does 'relative frequency' mean in a two-way table

Relative frequency converts a count into a proportion by dividing by the grand total (or a marginal total for conditional relative frequencies).

A school surveys 200 students about their preferred lunch option (Pizza or Salad) and their grade (Freshman or Senior). The results show 80 Freshmen chose Pizza, 20 Freshmen chose Salad, 50 Seniors chose Pizza, and 50 Seniors chose Salad. What is the joint frequency of Seniors who chose Salad

The joint frequency for Seniors choosing Salad is 50, as it is the count in the cell where the Senior row and Salad column intersect.

A hospital tracks patient satisfaction (Satisfied or Unsatisfied) by ward (A or B). Ward A: 70 Satisfied, 30 Unsatisfied. Ward B: 45 Satisfied, 55 Unsatisfied. What is the marginal frequency of satisfied patients

The marginal frequency of satisfied patients is the total of the Satisfied column: 70 + 45 = 115.

A marketing firm surveys 500 people about brand preference (Brand X or Brand Y) and age group (Under 30 or 30+). Under 30: 150 Brand X, 100 Brand Y. 30+: 80 Brand X, 170 Brand Y. What conditional frequency of Brand X preference exists for the Under 30 group

The conditional frequency of Brand X given Under 30 is 150 divided by the Under 30 total (150 + 100 = 250), which is 150/250 = 60%.
